---
title: "Private Repository & BOM Override"
tagline: "What to Do When You Need Enterprise Support"
type: scenario
tool: maven
---

#### ! Scenario What to Do When You Need Enterprise Support

This scenario shows how to incorporate a private, third-paty repository that provides patched or extended-support versions of dependencies.

It specifically demonstrates how a third-party Bill of Materials (BOM) can be used to override a publicly available, EOL (End-of-Life) dependency with a patched version from the private repository.

This is a common enterprise pattern when a vendor provides long-term support for a library (like Spring Boot) after its official EOL date.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    subgraph Public Repos
        SpringBoot["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14 (EOL)"]
    end
    subgraph Private Repo
        PatchedStarter["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14.ACME"]
        VendorBOM["com.thirdparty.springboot:patched-bom:1.0.0"]
        VendorBOM -. manages .-> PatchedStarter
    end
    Root[Project Root] -- imports --> VendorBOM
    Root --> SpringBoot_Declared("spring-boot-starter (version from BOM)")

```

The `pom.xml` first adds the private repository. Then, it imports the vendor's BOM in the `<dependencyManagement>` section. The actual dependency is declared without a version, letting the imported BOM control which version is used.

```{.xml include="fake-pom.xml" snippet="s16-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Adding a private repository and importing its BOM ensures that patched or extended-support versions are prioritized over public, EOL versions.

#### ! Why
Private repositories make custom artifacts available, while `<dependencyManagement>` (via a vendor BOM) enforces their selection. This combination allows for surgical overrides of public dependencies with enterprise-supported versions.

#### ! Takeaway
Adding a repository makes its artifacts *available*, but it is the combination of the repository and a version control mechanism (like a BOM in `<dependencyManagement>`) that allows you to safely override public dependencies with private, patched versions. The order of repository declaration can also matter if the same artifact exists in multiple repositories.
